title: Q-Learning Tic Tac Toe (Self-Play)
summary: tabular Q-learning -> two agents play each other -> Bellman update
description: Two tabular Q-learners trained against each other via self-play. Both use epsilon-greedy over a dict mapping (state, action) to estimated value, with linear epsilon decay. After each move, the opponent's prior state/action is updated with the resulting next-board, so both agents learn from every non-terminal step. The agent serialized for this demo plays X.

demo.caption: Pick which agent to play against. Click a cell to move.

learning_method.title: The update rule
learning_method.formula: Q(s, a) <- Q(s, a) + alpha * [r + gamma * max_a' Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]
points: alpha (learning rate) controls how much the new estimate overrides the old
gamma (discount) shrinks the value of rewards that are further in the future
max over a' is what makes Q-learning off-policy: the target assumes optimal future play, not the policy actually being followed
on a terminal transition the future term is forced to 0 because no reward remains
self-play: each agent's move is buffered for one turn so the opponent's response can form next_state before the Bellman update runs

what_i_learned: Self-play produces stronger adversarial states than a random opponent — the Q-table covers positions a random player would rarely reach.
Each agent needs its OWN buffered (state, action), not a single shared one — they update on different turns.
Reward is zero-sum: when one agent wins, the other's last move must be updated with -1 against its stashed state.
things_to_try_next: Evaluate with epsilon=0 against a perfect minimax player to measure true convergence (expect 100% draws).
Symmetry reduction: tic-tac-toe has 8-way symmetry, so collapsing equivalent states would shrink the Q-table ~8x.
Move to Connect Four or a larger board where tabular storage breaks and function approximation (DQN) becomes necessary.
